expanded_description: A trapezoid is a quadrilateral with exactly one pair of parallel sides, called the bases. The other two sides are the legs. An isosceles trapezoid has congruent legs and possesses additional properties: base angles are congruent, and diagonals are congruent. The midsegment of any trapezoid connects the midpoints of the legs, is parallel to the bases, and equals half the sum of the base lengths. These properties make trapezoids distinct from parallelograms and useful in architecture, design, and coordinate geometry.

core_concept.principle: A trapezoid is a quadrilateral with exactly one pair of parallel sides—the bases. The midsegment joins the midpoints of the legs; it is parallel to the bases, and its length is the average of the base lengths: $m = \frac{b_1 + b_2}{2}$. In an isosceles trapezoid, the legs are congruent, each pair of base angles is congruent, and the diagonals are congruent.
core_concept.explanation: Think of a trapezoid as a triangle with its top cut off parallel to the base. The cut creates two parallel bases of different lengths. The midsegment runs between the midpoints of the legs, perfectly parallel to the bases, and its length is exactly halfway between the two base lengths—it's the average. An isosceles trapezoid is symmetric: if you fold it along its midline, the legs match up. This symmetry forces the angles at each base to be equal, and the diagonals to be equal too. Unlike parallelograms, trapezoids have only one pair of parallel sides, which gives them a distinct set of properties.

image.alt: A trapezoid with bases b₁ and b₂ labeled, legs labeled, midsegment drawn with m=(b₁+b₂)/2. An isosceles trapezoid with congruent legs, base angles, and diagonals marked.
image.caption: Trapezoid: midsegment m = (b₁+b₂)/2. Isosceles trapezoid: legs ≅, base angles ≅, diagonals ≅.

sections.introduction: A trapezoid is defined by having exactly one pair of parallel sides. This single pair of parallels gives rise to the midsegment—a segment whose length is the average of the two base lengths, making it one of the most useful tools for solving trapezoid problems.

introduction_image.alt: A trapezoid with bases b₁ and b₂ highlighted. Legs labeled. Midsegment drawn connecting midpoints of legs, length m.
introduction_image.caption: Trapezoid: one pair of parallel sides (bases), two legs, and a midsegment.

key_concept.definition: A trapezoid is a quadrilateral with exactly one pair of parallel sides, called the bases. The non-parallel sides are the legs. The midsegment (or median) connects the midpoints of the legs. Midsegment Theorem: The midsegment is parallel to both bases, and its length is the average of the base lengths: $m = \frac{b_1 + b_2}{2}$.
key_concept.context: The midsegment theorem is proved by drawing a diagonal and using the triangle midsegment theorem on each resulting triangle. It holds for ALL trapezoids. Given any two of the three values (b₁, b₂, m), the third can be found.

image.alt: Trapezoid with diagonal drawn, dividing into two triangles. Midsegment shown as sum of two triangle midsegments. Formula derived.
image.caption: Midsegment Theorem: m = (b₁+b₂)/2, proved via triangle midsegments.

process_flow.process: Identify the Bases, Legs, and Midsegment
process_flow.description: Determine which sides are the parallel bases and which are the legs. The midsegment connects the midpoints of the legs.
process_flow.mechanism: Look for parallel marks. The two parallel sides are bases. The other two sides are legs. Find midpoints of legs to locate midsegment endpoints. If the midsegment is given, it is the segment joining those midpoints.
process_flow.result: Bases b₁ and b₂ identified; legs and midsegment located.
applications: '$\overline{AB} \parallel \overline{CD}$ → AB and CD are bases; AD and BC are legs.'

image.alt: Trapezoid with bases and legs labeled. Midpoints of legs marked. Midsegment drawn.
image.caption: Step 1: Identify the bases, legs, and midsegment.

process_flow.description: Use $m = (b_1 + b_2)/2$ to find the unknown among the three values.
process_flow.mechanism: If bases are known: $m = (b_1+b_2)/2$. If m and one base known: $b_2 = 2m - b_1$. The midsegment is also parallel to both bases.
process_flow.result: Unknown base or midsegment length found.

image.alt: Trapezoid with formula applied. Example calculations shown.
image.caption: Step 2: m = (b₁+b₂)/2. Solve for the unknown.

image.alt: Complete trapezoid section with definition, midsegment theorem, and examples.
image.caption: The Trapezoid and Its Midsegment overview.

sections.introduction: An isosceles trapezoid adds one condition: the legs are congruent. This symmetry produces base angle congruence and diagonal congruence. Isosceles trapezoids are the most symmetric trapezoids.

introduction_image.alt: An isosceles trapezoid with congruent legs marked, congruent base angles marked, and congruent diagonals drawn.
introduction_image.caption: Isosceles trapezoid: legs ≅, base angles ≅, diagonals ≅.

key_concept.definition: An isosceles trapezoid is a trapezoid with congruent legs. Theorem 1: Base angles of an isosceles trapezoid are congruent—each pair of angles sharing a base is equal. Theorem 2: The diagonals of an isosceles trapezoid are congruent. These three conditions (congruent legs, congruent base angles, congruent diagonals) are equivalent in a trapezoid.
key_concept.context: The base angle theorem applies to both bases. Angles adjacent to each leg are supplementary (bases are parallel). The diagonal congruence is proved using SAS on triangles formed by the diagonals. The midsegment formula still applies.

image.alt: Isosceles trapezoid with legs, base angles, and diagonals marked. Proof sketch for diagonal congruence.
image.caption: Isosceles trapezoid properties: equivalent conditions and proofs.

process_flow.description: Verify legs are congruent, base angles are congruent, or diagonals are congruent.
process_flow.mechanism: Any of these three, combined with one pair of parallel sides, guarantees the trapezoid is isosceles.

image.alt: Three ways to identify an isosceles trapezoid.
image.caption: Step 1: Confirm the trapezoid is isosceles.

process_flow.description: Use base angle congruence and diagonal congruence to find unknown measures.
process_flow.mechanism: Base angles on same base are equal. Angles adjacent to a leg sum to 180°. Diagonals are equal: set expressions equal and solve.
process_flow.result: Angle measures or diagonal lengths found.
applications: One base angle 70° → other on same base 70°, angles on other base 110° each.

image.alt: Isosceles trapezoid with base angles and diagonals used in equations.
image.caption: Step 2: Apply base angle and diagonal properties.

image.alt: Complete isosceles trapezoid section with properties and proofs.
image.caption: The Isosceles Trapezoid overview.

sections.introduction: Apply trapezoid and isosceles trapezoid properties to find midsegments, base lengths, angle measures, and diagonal lengths.

worked_examples.title: Trapezoid Problems
worked_examples.problem:
1) Bases 12 and 20. Find midsegment.
2) Midsegment = 15, one base = 9. Find other base.
3) Isosceles trapezoid: base angle 65°. Find all angles.
4) Isosceles trapezoid: PR = 4x−2, QS = 2x+10. Find PR.
5) Prove base angles of an isosceles trapezoid are congruent.

step_by_step.explanation: Draw altitudes from top base to bottom base. Right triangles formed are congruent by HL (legs congruent, altitudes equal). Corresponding base angles are congruent by CPCTC.

image.caption: Trapezoid problems: midsegment, bases, angles, diagonals, proof.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Midsegment theorem, isosceles trapezoid angle and diagonal properties.

summary: A trapezoid has exactly one pair of parallel sides (bases). Midsegment: $m = (b_1+b_2)/2$, parallel to bases. Isosceles trapezoid: legs congruent ↔ base angles congruent ↔ diagonals congruent. Midsegment formula applies to all trapezoids.

review_questions_image.alt: Concept map with Trapezoid in center, branches for midsegment, bases, legs, isosceles properties.
review_questions_image.caption: Properties of Trapezoids and Isosceles Trapezoids Review.
